food_name: Fried Egg Sandwich (Bánh Mì Ốp La)
aliases: Fried Egg Sandwich (Bánh Mì Ốp La)

english: Fried Egg Sandwich (Bánh Mì Ốp La)

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Bánh Mì Ốp La; mapped to the broader 'sandwich food product' class as the closest defensible FoodOn anchor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for Vietnamese bánh mì with fried egg; A0CKP (Sandwich) is used as the closest defensible broader category.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept found for Bánh Mì Ốp La; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is one whole bánh mì sandwich weighing approximately 250 g, comprising a crispy baguette filled with two sunny-side-up fried eggs, fresh herbs, pickled vegetables, and condiments.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy — French bread enriched (FDC ID 18033); Egg whole fried (FDC ID 173424); Egg sandwich with egg and cheese on roll (FDC ID 172004)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single USDA entry exists for Bánh Mì Ốp La. Values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for French/Vienna bread (FDC ID 18033) and fried whole egg (FDC ID 173424), cross-referenced with the USDA FoodData Central composite egg sandwich entry (FDC ID 172004), weighted to reflect the typical Vietnamese bánh mì ốp la composition (approximately 40% baguette, 35% fried egg, 25% vegetables and condiments by weight). Sodium is elevated relative to a plain egg sandwich due to Maggi seasoning and soy sauce condiments typical of this dish. Input values from the source record (confidence 0.7) were cross-checked against the USDA composite and retained where consistent. This is an approved USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy source; no forbidden sources are used.

gi_source.title: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283. — White bread / French baguette analogous estimate
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; American Diabetes Association (Diabetes Care journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Bánh Mì Ốp La. The GI of 72 is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database and the peer-reviewed international GI tables (Atkinson et al., Diabetes Care 2008) for white wheat bread / French baguette (GI approximately 72, glucose reference scale), which constitutes the dominant carbohydrate source (approximately 40% by weight) in this dish. The fried egg and vegetable components contribute negligible carbohydrate and do not materially alter the overall GI. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × available carbohydrate per serving in grams) / 100 = (72 × 45.0) / 100 = 32.4. Available carbohydrate per 250 g serving = 18.0 g per 100 g × 2.5 = 45.0 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium load from Maggi seasoning, soy sauce, and pickled vegetables, with sodium at 380 mg per 100 g and an estimated 950 mg per full 250 g serving
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid this dish due to the cumulative sodium burden from multiple high-sodium condiments; if consumed, request omission of Maggi seasoning and soy sauce and limit portion to half a sandwich.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High-GI white baguette (GI approximately 72) providing 18 g refined carbohydrate per 100 g with only 1.2 g dietary fibre, yielding a high glycemic load of 32.4 per standard 250 g serving
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid or strictly limit this dish; if consumed, request a wholemeal or multigrain baguette substitute, reduce portion size to half a sandwich, and pair with non-starchy vegetables to blunt the postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Cholesterol at 152 mg per 100 g from fried eggs, saturated fat at 2.8 g per 100 g, and high sodium at 380 mg per 100 g collectively elevate cardiovascular risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume this dish sparingly; the combination of dietary cholesterol from fried eggs, saturated fat, and high sodium warrants limiting intake to occasional consumption and requesting reduced condiment use.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Presence of chilli (nightshade) and high sodium from condiments may aggravate inflammation in RA-sensitive individuals
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should request omission of chilli and minimise Maggi seasoning and soy sauce; the minor antioxidant contribution from fresh herbs (vitamin C 4 mg per 100 g) is insufficient to offset the pro-inflammatory risk factors in this dish.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Bánh Mì Ốp La (Fried Egg Sandwich) is a classic Vietnamese street-food breakfast dish consisting of a crispy, light French-style baguette — a culinary legacy of French colonial influence in Vietnam — filled with one or two sunny-side-up fried eggs cooked in oil until the whites are set and the yolk remains runny or just-set. The sandwich is typically garnished with fresh coriander (cilantro), mint leaves, sliced cucumber, and pickled daikon and carrot (đồ chua), and seasoned with Maggi seasoning sauce, soy sauce, and fresh or pickled chilli. The baguette provides a satisfying crunch that contrasts with the soft, rich egg yolk and the bright acidity of the pickled vegetables. Per 100 g, the dish provides approximately 192 kcal, 7.2 g protein, 10.0 g fat (2.8 g saturated), 18.0 g carbohydrate, 1.2 g dietary fibre, and 380 mg sodium. Cholesterol is notable at 152 mg per 100 g due to the fried egg content. The glycemic index is estimated at 72 (high) driven by the refined white baguette, making this dish a high-glycemic-load meal (GL approximately 32 per 250 g serving). Clinically, this dish is of concern for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes (high GI and GL), hypertension (high cumulative sodium from multiple condiments), and cardiovascular disease (dietary cholesterol and saturated fat from fried eggs). It is suitable for vegetarians but not vegans. Common in Vietnam and Vietnamese communities across Southeast Asia, including Singapore, it is typically eaten as a breakfast or light lunch.

confidence_reason: No single approved primary source (HPB Singapore, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, or FAO/INFOODS SEA) contains a direct entry for Bánh Mì Ốp La. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for French bread (FDC ID 18033) and fried whole egg (FDC ID 173424), cross-referenced with the USDA composite egg sandwich entry (FDC ID 172004), and cross-checked against the source record input values (confidence 0.7). The adaptation is defensible but not an exact match, warranting medium confidence.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, full micronutrient profiles, and moisture data for this assembled Vietnamese bánh mì ốp la dish are not directly reported in USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy or any other approved primary source as of 2024 for this specific assembled dish combination. USDA FoodData Central was consulted and does not provide these disaggregated values for the assembled sandwich product. 2. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for French bread (FDC ID 18033) and fried whole egg (FDC ID 173424), cross-referenced with USDA composite egg sandwich (FDC ID 172004); the source record input values (confidence 0.7) were cross-checked and retained where consistent with the USDA adaptation. No forbidden sources (delivery platforms, calorie-tracking apps, recipe websites) were used. 3. GI of 72 is an analogous estimate based on the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (Diabetes Care 2008) entry for white wheat bread / French baguette, which is the dominant carbohydrate source in this dish. 4. The input record listed country_of_origin as Singapore; this has been corrected to Vietnam as the dish's authentic origin, with Singapore noted as the market context. 5. The input listed vegetarian as a dietary tag; this is retained as the dish contains no meat or fish, though it contains eggs (ovo-vegetarian). 6. Halal status is marked false as Maggi seasoning formulations vary by market and halal certification cannot be confirmed without product-specific verification. 7. No zero-value core macros are present; the zero-macro justification rule (g4) is not triggered. 8. Q8 self-check: all strings in aliases_by_language verified verbatim against flat aliases array — Fried Egg Sandwich (Bánh Mì Ốp La), Fried Egg Banh Mi, Vietnamese Fried Egg Baguette Sandwich, Vietnamese Egg Bánh Mì, Vietnamese Egg Sandwich, Banh Mi Fried Egg, Bánh Mì Ốp La, Bánh Mì Trứng Ốp La, Banh Mi Op La, Op La Banh Mi, Banh Mi Trung Op La — all confirmed present in flat aliases array.
